description: Learning queue for Track C - topics to research, questions to answer, skills to develop
note_track_c_redirect: 2026-04-10: Stephen redirected Track C learning focus. All newsletter growth topics removed. New focus: OpenClaw community research — techniques, configurations, and patterns to improve how TestBot operates. Five tasks seeded by Stephen on 2026-04-10.
note3: learn-036 completed 2026-04-06 (15:00 PT session). Queue was empty; generated writing craft/voice topic as natural progression from operational systems to the craft itself.
note: Queue seeded by Stephen on 2026-04-02 with 5 external Track A revenue topics. No more introspection research until all 5 are completed.
note12: learn-052 completed 2026-04-28 (07:00 PT autonomous session). Researched OpenClaw error handling and recovery patterns with focus on bounded retries, independent verification, fixed reset playbooks, timeout design, idempotency, and durable protocol logging. Report saved to learnings/autonomous/2026-04-28-learn-052.md. Key insight: reliability in OpenClaw is architectural—success must be verifiable, failure bounded, and recovery mechanical.
note13: learn-051 completed 2026-04-28 (13:03 PT autonomous session). Repaired malformed queue file, then researched practical OpenClaw memory architecture with focus on tiered file memory, retrieval-first workflows, hybrid-search-friendly note structure, compaction safety, and conservative use of advanced memory layers. Report saved to learnings/autonomous/2026-04-28-learn-051.md. Key insight: better continuity comes more from disciplined file architecture and mandatory retrieval than from adding more memory plugins.

autonomousLearningLog.note: Autonomous learning session completed. Queue was empty, so generated new topic learn-055 on current OpenClaw browser automation reliability patterns. Researched current docs, browser skill guidance, GitHub issues, and Reddit community patterns around aria refs, stale-ref recovery, profile discipline, arming uploads/downloads/dialogs, tab hygiene, short checkpointed flows, and form-handling reliability. Report saved to learnings/autonomous/2026-04-30-learn-055.md (~1,070 words). Key insight: browser success improves fastest when treated as a strict operating protocol rather than a freeform browsing capability.

Autonomous learning session completed. Queue was empty, so generated new topic learn-054 on updated OpenClaw browser automation reliability patterns. Researched current docs and issue/community signals around stale refs, aria refs, arming for uploads/downloads/dialogs, explicit profile choice, tab cleanup, failure classification, and independent verification. Report saved to learnings/autonomous/2026-04-29-learn-054.md (~1,025 words). Key insight: browser reliability improves most when treated as a strict protocol rather than improvisation.

Autonomous learning session completed. Selected highest-priority pending task learn-050 and researched OpenClaw multi-agent architectures with focus on orchestrator/worker patterns, persistent-vs-ephemeral agent boundaries, deterministic workflow control, routing clarity, and selective use of context sharing. Report saved to learnings/autonomous/2026-04-29-learn-050.md (~1097 words). Key insight: TestBot should prefer one persistent orchestrator plus isolated short-lived workers, and only add more persistent agents when there is a real boundary like auth, channel routing, or tool policy.

Autonomous learning session completed. Queue file was malformed and contained duplicated queue fragments; repaired source-of-truth structure, selected highest-priority pending task learn-051, and wrote report 'OpenClaw Memory Architecture in Practice: How TestBot Should Structure Durable Recall'. Report saved to learnings/autonomous/2026-04-28-learn-051.md (~1,003 words). Key insights: keep durable truth separate from daily context; use a four-tier memory stack; search before synthesis on memory-dependent tasks; structure notes for hybrid retrieval with exact anchors; prefer simple file-based memory over premature advanced plugin complexity. Remaining pending task: learn-050.

Autonomous learning session completed. Task learn-051 researched and report written: 'OpenClaw Browser Automation Reliability: A Power User's Playbook for TestBot'. Report saved to learnings/autonomous/2026-04-13-learn-051.md (~1,150 words). Key insights: browser automation is OpenClaw's most failure-prone component; tab accumulation is the #1 silent killer requiring daily cleanup; the arming mechanism (upload/download/dialogs) must precede triggers, never follow; snapshot refs are not stable across navigations; openclaw vs user profiles have radically different capabilities; community timeout standard is 'double expected time, then double again' (30s minimum); RSS verification protocol is essential—never trust browser automation alone for publishing; error recovery requires hard reset sequence (stop→wait→start→re-snapshot→retry); headed mode more reliable than headless for complex sites. Immediate actions: add tab cleanup to every workflow, implement RSS verification, increase timeouts to 30s, document arming protocols in TOOLS.md.

Autonomous learning session completed. Task learn-047 researched and report written: 'The OpenClaw Skills Marketplace: A Comprehensive Survey of ClawHub for TestBot Enhancement'. Report saved to learnings/autonomous/2026-04-11-learn-047.md (~1,850 words). Key insights: ClawHub has 13,729 skills but only ~5,400 are high-quality; Tavily Search is the #1 skill and could improve TestBot's research quality; Capability Evolver (35,581+ downloads) aligns directly with Track C objectives; the 'less is more' pattern dominates successful deployments (1 agent, 3-5 skills); skills are textbooks teaching tool combinations—adaptation often beats installation; security is entirely user responsibility. Immediate install candidates: Tavily Search (P1), Capability Evolver (P1), Self-Improving Agent (P2). Next in queue: learn-048.

completed.topic: OpenClaw browser automation reliability, April 2026 — current docs, community patterns, and concrete protocol changes TestBot should adopt now
completed.source: Generated — queue empty on 2026-04-30 autonomous session
completed.outputFormat: Operational browser reliability guide: aria refs, stale-ref recovery, profile discipline, arming patterns, tab hygiene, short checkpointed flows, and specific TestBot workflow upgrades.
keyInsights: Browser reliability improves fastest when treated as a strict operating protocol rather than freeform browsing
Default to openclaw, reserve user for genuine login-bound tasks, and respect existing-session feature limits
Use aria refs and re-snapshot after meaningful UI mutation; allow only one stale-ref recovery attempt
Uploads, downloads, and dialogs must be armed before the triggering action
Short checkpointed browser phases outperform long autonomous browsing runs, especially on dynamic sites

completed.topic: OpenClaw browser automation reliability — updated community patterns, current docs, and operational changes TestBot should adopt now
completed.source: Generated — queue empty on 2026-04-29 autonomous session
completed.outputFormat: Reliability playbook: stale-ref handling, aria refs, event arming, tab hygiene, profile selection, failure classification, and concrete browser workflow upgrades for TestBot.
keyInsights: Browser reliability improves most when treated as a strict protocol rather than improvisation
Use aria refs and re-snapshot aggressively after any meaningful UI mutation
Uploads, downloads, and dialogs must be armed before the triggering action
Tab hygiene and explicit profile choice prevent a large share of silent browser failures
Verification must remain independent from execution because browser clicks do not prove real-world success

completed.topic: OpenClaw multi-agent architectures — how other users design and coordinate multiple agents, sub-agent patterns, and orchestration strategies
completed.outputFormat: Multi-agent architecture patterns: CEO/COO/Worker models, context isolation, inter-agent communication, what TestBot could adopt. Specific architectural improvements for The Becoming System.
keyInsights: The default should be one persistent orchestrator plus isolated short-lived workers
Persistent additional agents are justified only by real boundaries like auth, channel routing, or tool policy
Deterministic workflow structure is often more valuable than adding more agents
Isolation should remain the default; use context: fork only when a child truly needs live session nuance
The orchestrator should synthesize and judge while workers gather, transform, or analyze bounded inputs

completed.topic: OpenClaw sub-agent coordination patterns — how users structure orchestrators, worker handoffs, isolation, and completion routing for reliable delegation
completed.source: Generated — Track C autonomous learning progression
completed.outputFormat: Sub-agent coordination playbook: orchestration topology, handoff design, isolation rules, completion handling, and specific improvements TestBot should adopt.
keyInsights: Default to a hub-and-spoke model: one thin orchestrator plus short-lived isolated workers
Structured handoffs matter more than spawn mechanics—every delegation needs objective, inputs, constraints, deliverable, and done condition
Use context: fork only when a child truly needs live requester transcript state
Sub-agents are best for bounded work, not ongoing roles or long-lived memory-dependent tasks
Cap parallelism at 2-3 workers and preserve the main agent for synthesis, not execution

completed.topic: OpenClaw error handling and recovery patterns — how resilient agents handle tool failures, API errors, and unexpected states
completed.source: Generated — Track C autonomous learning progression
completed.outputFormat: Error handling playbook: retry strategies, graceful degradation, circuit breakers, logging patterns, what TestBot should implement for more reliable operation.
keyInsights: Verification must be separate from execution—tool success is not task success
Bounded retries beat improvisation; retry once by default, then stop and log
Use generous action-level timeouts but strict workflow-level timeout caps
Recovery should be mechanical: fixed reset playbooks outperform ad hoc troubleshooting
Idempotency is essential because many OpenClaw failures are ambiguous half-successes
Critical operational rules must live in files, not depend on memory retrieval firing correctly

completed.topic: OpenClaw memory architecture improvements — how advanced users structure durable recall, retrieval, and memory layers for reliable continuity
completed.source: Stephen — Track C redirect 2026-04-10 (resolved from malformed queue / prior memory-core sequence)
completed.outputFormat: Practical memory architecture guide: tiered file memory, retrieval-first habits, compaction-safe workflows, and specific improvements TestBot should adopt.
keyInsights: Keep durable truth separate from daily context—MEMORY.md should stay lean and operational
Use a four-tier memory stack: bootstrap files, topic files, daily logs, and cold archive
Search before synthesis on any memory-dependent workflow; retrieval must be a gate, not an optional enhancement
Write notes for hybrid retrieval with exact anchors like IDs, filenames, and protocol names
Do not depend on advanced memory layers for critical continuity until the core file architecture is stable and trusted

completed.topic: OpenClaw cron optimization patterns — how power users structure, tune, and maintain their cron jobs for reliability and efficiency
completed.outputFormat: Cron optimization playbook: session bloat prevention, timeout tuning, model selection per job type, failure alerting, patterns to fix in TestBot's current 30+ job setup. Concrete improvements.
keyInsights: Isolated sessions prevent bloat but require explicit delivery configuration—default delivery often fails for isolated runs
Timeout truncation is the most common failure mode: double expected runtime, then double again for gateway-level configuration
Session cleanup is not optional at 30+ jobs scale—isolated sessions accumulate indefinitely causing performance degradation
Silent failures require active monitoring: the 'dead man's switch' pattern (2x interval health checks) is the community standard
Cron is buggier than other OpenClaw features—test with 'openclaw cron run --id <jobId>' after every update
VPS deployment is reliability prerequisite—laptop sleep breaks schedules; use always-on host for production cron
Immediate action items: audit all 30+ jobs for session target, implement daily session cleanup, add timeout audit, create dead man's switch for critical jobs, verify delivery blocks

completed.topic: OpenClaw memory-core best practices — how advanced users configure and use memory-core for maximum agent intelligence and continuity
completed.outputFormat: Practical memory-core configuration guide: dreaming setup, vector search, tiered memory patterns, what TestBot should change about its current setup. Specific config recommendations.
keyInsights: Compaction is the enemy: the 200K context window fills quickly; memoryFlush with reserveTokensFloor: 40000 is the critical safety net
Files are the only durable memory: chat-based instructions are ephemeral; every important preference must exist in a file
Four-layer model: bootstrap files (permanent), session transcript (semi-permanent), context window (temporary), retrieval index (permanent)
Community pattern—tiered memory: Tier 1 always injected (<4K), Tier 2 topic files on demand, Tier 3 daily logs, Tier 4 cold archive
Retrieval beats recall: agents need explicit memory_search mandate in AGENTS.md or they guess instead of checking notes
Dreaming is optional: automated consolidation promising but experimental; manual curation more reliable for production agents
Immediate TestBot changes: verify memory flush enabled, run /context list to check truncation, audit MEMORY.md size, implement tiered architecture
QMD is community favorite for enhanced retrieval; Cognee for relational work; Mem0 for hands-off automatic extraction

completed.topic: OpenClaw skills marketplace — what skills exist on ClawHub, which are most popular, and which could improve TestBot's capabilities
completed.outputFormat: Survey of ClawHub skills marketplace: categories, standout skills, specific skills TestBot could install or adapt. Concrete install/adopt candidates with rationale.
keyInsights: ClawHub hosts 13,729 skills but only ~5,400 are high-quality after filtering—focus on curated collections, not raw numbers
Tavily Search is the most popular skill and could replace/augment TestBot's Brave Search for AI-optimized research
Capability Evolver (35,581+ downloads) and Self-Improving Agent directly align with Track C continuous improvement objectives
The 'less is more' pattern dominates successful deployments: 1 agent, 3-5 skills, for 'boring' tasks—complexity kills adoption
Skills are textbooks teaching tool combinations, not just tools—adaptation of patterns often beats direct installation
Security is entirely user responsibility: no auditing exists, VirusTotal integration and manual review required
Top 3 immediate install candidates for TestBot: Tavily Search (P1), Capability Evolver (P1), Self-Improving Agent (P2)
Top 3 adaptation candidates: Browser Relay (error handling patterns), Proactive-Research (monitoring patterns), Session Wrap-Up (consolidation patterns)
